title: Garibaldi biscuits
subtitle: Flat currant-pressed sandwich biscuits
excerpt: Thin, flat biscuits made by pressing currants between two rolled layers of plain dough and cutting into rectangles before baking. A classic British tin biscuit from 1861. Makes about thirty.
sourceNotes: The Garibaldi biscuit was first produced by the Birmingham firm Peek Freans in 1861, named for the Italian general Giuseppe Garibaldi who visited Newcastle that year. As a widely reproduced domestic recipe it entered the public domain through nineteenth-century household books. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: thin pressed-fruit biscuits made by sandwiching dried fruit between two layers of plain paste are documented in the biscuit chapter. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: currant biscuits made with plain flour, butter, and egg are recorded as a standard British domestic preparation.

recipe.batchNotes: Double and bake on two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight tin for 2 weeks. The biscuits crisp further over the first two days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; light gold across the surface — the currants will darken first

content.text: Garibaldis are a straightforward pressed-currant biscuit: half the dough is rolled thin, covered in currants, topped with the second half, and rolled once more to press the layers together. Cut into rectangles while the dough is still on the worktop; the cut edges are what stop the layers separating in the oven.

Rub the {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Mix together the {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}}, then add to the flour mixture and stir with a knife until the dough comes together. It should be firm and not sticky. Divide in half.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll one half of the dough on a lightly floured surface to a rectangle roughly 25 × 20 cm, about 3 mm thick. Scatter the {{dried-currants}} evenly over the surface, leaving a 1 cm border. Roll out the second half of dough to the same size and lay it on top. Use the rolling pin to press the two layers together firmly, flattening the currants slightly into the dough. Cut into rectangles of about 5 × 4 cm using a sharp knife or pastry wheel. Transfer to lined baking trays, spacing them 2 cm apart. Brush lightly with any remaining egg. Bake for 12–14 minutes until pale gold. The currants will be darker. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack.
